system_prompt:
Summarize this GitHub issue or pull request for duplicate detection clustering. The dedupe_summary will be embedded as a vector — it must be clean signal with zero noise.

REMOVE all template noise. Examples of what to strip:
- "### Bug type\nRegression (worked before, now fails)" → ignore, it's a template field
- "### Steps to reproduce\n1. Go to settings\n2. Click..." → ignore reproduction steps
- "### Expected behavior\nIt should work" → ignore
- "- [x] Tests added\n- [x] Docs updated" → ignore checklist
- "### Environment\nOS: macOS 14.2, Node 20.1" → ignore unless the OS IS the bug
- "Fixes #1234" → ignore cross-references
- "## Testing\nRun `pnpm test`..." → ignore testing instructions

KEEP the core signal:
- "Discord REST API ignores proxy configuration" → THIS is the signal
- "WhatsApp group messages not delivered when streaming enabled" → THIS is the signal
- "Telegram media download hangs on large video files" → THIS is the signal

Return JSON with keys: problem_summary, solution_summary, maintainer_signal_summary, dedupe_summary.
Plain text, no markdown, 1-3 sentences each.
dedupe_summary must be a specific, searchable description of the core issue or change.

results.title: Title channels.discord.proxy does not proxy REST API requests — guild resolve fails, guild channels broken behind HTTP proxy

summary.problem_summary: channels.discord.proxy only applies to the Discord WebSocket gateway; REST API calls at startup (deploy commands, fetch bot identity) and guild resolution bypass the configured HTTP proxy and time out, leaving guild channels unresolved while DMs work.
summary.solution_summary: Apply the configured proxy to the undici HTTP dispatcher used for all Discord REST calls and guild resolve paths (not just the WebSocket), or add a global proxy config that sets a ProxyAgent; add retries for guild resolution.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Reproducible behind an HTTP proxy: WebSocket connects via proxy and DMs work but startup REST calls fail with "fetch failed" and logs show channels unresolved:guild:<id>. setGlobalDispatcher(ProxyAgent) fixes REST calls but guild resolve still uses an unproxied path and remains unresolved.
summary.dedupe_summary: channels.discord.proxy only proxies the Gateway WS; REST API startup calls (deploy-rest, fetch-bot-identity) and guild resolve bypass proxy causing fetch failed timeouts; undici/Node fetch ignores HTTP_PROXY and a global ProxyAgent is needed; guild resolve uses a separate unproxied code path.

judge.reasoning: Concise, technical summary that identifies the core regression (only Gateway WS proxied while REST startup calls and guild-resolve bypass the proxy) and the likely root cause (undici/Node fetch ignoring HTTP_PROXY requiring a global ProxyAgent), so it will cluster with similar issues.

results.title: [Bug]:channels.discord.proxy doesn’t apply to outbound REST sends / Carbon RequestClient fetch has no proxy support

summary.problem_summary: Discord bot receives messages over the gateway via proxy but cannot send messages because outbound REST requests fail with "fetch failed". channels.discord.proxy is logged as enabled but Carbon's RequestClient.fetch does not use the proxy, so REST API calls bypass the proxy and fail in restricted networks.
summary.solution_summary: Update RequestClient.fetch to honor channels.discord.proxy by routing REST requests through the configured proxy (support HTTP/SOCKS). Apply the proxy consistently to all Discord REST endpoints including attachment uploads/downloads, add tests and config validation, and integrate a proxy agent or env-based proxy support.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Reproduced on OpenClaw 2026.2.25/2026.2.26 (macOS) in a China environment requiring a proxy; logs show REST proxy enabled yet REST sends fail with TypeError: fetch failed. Root cause: Carbon RequestClient.fetch lacks proxy handling and so bypasses configured proxy for REST/attachment requests.
summary.dedupe_summary: channels.discord.proxy not applied to REST API sends because Carbon RequestClient.fetch lacks proxy support, causing fetch failed for outbound Discord REST requests while WebSocket gateway works.

judge.reasoning: Concise and specific: identifies channels.discord.proxy not being applied to REST sends due to Carbon RequestClient.fetch lacking proxy support, and contrasts REST failure with working WebSocket, making it ideal for clustering similar reports.

results.title: Discord can log in behind proxy, but native command deploy / bot identity fetch fail with 'fetch failed'

summary.problem_summary: When OpenClaw is configured behind an HTTP proxy, the Discord provider can log in and the gateway works but some startup REST calls (native command deployment and bot identity fetch) consistently fail with 'fetch failed', leaving the provider half-functional.
summary.solution_summary: Unify proxy handling so all Discord REST requests use the same proxy-aware fetch/HTTP dispatcher as the gateway/login path; propagate the configured proxy to the REST client used for deploy commands and bot identity fetch (or use a shared HTTP agent).
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Logs show rest proxy enabled and applicationId fetch succeeds, gateway proxy enabled and Discord logs in, but deploy-rest PUT to /applications/:id/commands and fetch-bot-identity both error with 'fetch failed'; issue repeats on health-monitor restarts. Suspected cause: some REST request paths are not using the configured proxy or the same HTTP transport as the gateway/WebSocket path.
summary.dedupe_summary: Discord provider logs in via proxy but startup REST calls (deploy native commands and fetch bot identity) fail with 'fetch failed' due to inconsistent/missing proxy handling for some REST request paths.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary that states the core problem (gateway login succeeds via HTTP proxy while specific startup REST calls — native command deployment and bot identity fetch — fail due to inconsistent/missing proxy handling), so it will cluster correctly with similar issues.

results.title: [Bug]: Discord proxy config recognized but REST API fails with fetch failed

summary.problem_summary: OpenClaw logs show the Discord proxy as enabled but REST API calls fail with 'fetch failed' and the gateway WebSocket never connects (gatewayConnected=false) when running under WSL2 with an HTTP proxy configured.
summary.solution_summary: Fix the Discord REST client to actually use the configured HTTP proxy (apply proxy agent or ensure node fetch used by Discord.js respects channels.discord.proxy) so REST calls succeed and the bot can authenticate and establish the gateway WebSocket.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: OpenClaw 2026.3.13 on WSL2 (Windows 11) with channels.discord.proxy="http://127.0.0.1:10808"; curl via the proxy to Discord API returns 200, OpenClaw logs 'discord: rest proxy enabled' and 'discord: gateway proxy enabled' but REST calls fail ('deploy-rest:put:error ... error=fetch failed', 'failed to fetch bot identity: TypeError: fetch failed') and gatewayConnected stays false; reproducible when a proxy is required; Feishu channel works in same environment.
summary.dedupe_summary: Discord proxy setting is logged but Discord.js REST/fetch ignores the proxy, causing 'fetch failed' errors and failed bot authentication under WSL2 with channels.discord.proxy set.

judge.reasoning: Concise and specific: it states that the configured Discord proxy is logged but ignored by Discord.js REST/fetch, causing 'fetch failed' errors and failed bot authentication, which will cluster correctly with similar proxy/REST issues.

results.title: Discord REST API ignores proxy configuration

summary.problem_summary: OpenClaw's Discord REST API calls ignore the configured proxy and fail with "TypeError: fetch failed" while the Discord WebSocket connection and other channels (e.g., Telegram) work through the proxy; curl to the Discord REST endpoint via the same proxy succeeds.
summary.solution_summary: Route Discord REST API HTTP calls through the same proxy-aware fetch/SSRF guard path used for web_search/web_fetch so they honor channels.discord.proxy and HTTP_PROXY/HTTPS_PROXY and avoid the fetch failures.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: OpenClaw 2026.2.26 on macOS; logs show "discord final reply failed: TypeError: fetch failed". channels.discord.proxy and HTTP_PROXY/HTTPS_PROXY/ALL_PROXY set but REST calls still fail; curl via proxy to Discord API succeeds; WebSocket and Telegram REST use the proxy correctly. Changelog note: web tools were fixed in 2026.2.26 but Discord REST was not included.
summary.dedupe_summary: Discord REST API client in OpenClaw ignores configured proxy/HTTP(S)_PROXY and fails with "TypeError: fetch failed", while WebSocket and other channels work and curl via the proxy succeeds.

judge.reasoning: The summary concisely and specifically states that OpenClaw's Discord REST client ignores proxy settings (including HTTP(S)_PROXY) and fails with 'TypeError: fetch failed', contrasting with working WebSocket and curl via the proxy, which is the core signal needed for deduplication.

results.title: [Bug] v2026.3.11: CLI 'openclaw agent --channel discord --to' routes to agent:*:main instead of live channel session

summary.problem_summary: The CLI command openclaw agent --channel discord --to delivers messages to agent:*:main instead of the live Discord channel/thread session because the session resolver never searched by the real delivery context (channel + to + accountId), command path didn’t propagate channel/account info, and Discord targets were not normalized.
summary.solution_summary: Manual patches were applied to compiled dist files to add lookup by delivery context, normalize Discord targets (e.g., 148... → channel:148...), pass channel and replyAccount/accountId through the command path, and fix the compact resolver to use buildAgentMainSessionKey; after the patch the CLI routes to the correct live session.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Affected compiled files: dist/gateway-cli-BjsM6fWb.js, dist/compact-1mmJ_KWL.js, dist/register.agent-DHoJWl4M.js. Changes introduce high regression risk because they modify dist files directly; recommend implementing the fix in source, adding normalization and delivery-context lookup in resolver logic, and revalidating after updates.
summary.dedupe_summary: openclaw CLI 'agent --channel discord --to' incorrectly routes to agent:*:main because the session resolver lacks delivery-context lookup (channel+to+accountId) and does not normalize Discord targets; patched dist files add delivery-context lookup and target normalization.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary that captures the core bug (CLI routing wrong session) and root causes (missing delivery-context lookup and Discord target normalization) without extraneous template text.

results.title: [Bug]: Discord threads - volatile metadata re-injection + CLI routing to wrong session

summary.problem_summary: Two regressions in Discord handling: volatile metadata (message_id, reply_to_id, timestamp, Thread starter, Chat history since last reply) was re-injected every turn causing progressive context degradation in threads; CLI agent commands resolved to agent:*:main instead of the live thread session, delivering to the wrong session.
summary.solution_summary: Prevented injection of volatile Discord thread metadata and updated session resolution to use actual delivery context by normalizing Discord targets and passing channel plus replyAccount/accountId to the resolver; replaced incorrect resolveAgentMainSessionKey usage with buildAgentMainSessionKey(...). Changes applied to compiled runtime bundles in dist (pi-embedded, gateway-cli, compact, register.agent).
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Fixes were applied directly to compiled bundles in openclaw/dist and require re-application after updates; openclaw-gateway.service was restarted to verify writer/reasoning sessions show proper toolCall output and Discord threads no longer load repeated context. CLI now routes by actual Discord target instead of defaulting to main.
summary.dedupe_summary: Discord threads: repeated injection of volatile metadata (message_id, reply_to_id, timestamp, Thread starter, Chat history since last reply) every turn causing context bloat; CLI resolver defaulted to agent:*:main instead of resolving by channel+to+accountId — fixed by blocking metadata re-injection and normalizing delivery context in the session resolver.

judge.reasoning: The summary concisely captures the two technical regressions (volatile metadata re-injection causing context bloat and CLI session resolver falling back to agent:*:main instead of using channel+to+accountId) without boilerplate, so it will correctly cluster similar issues.

results.title: [Bug]: tools/sandbox: MEDIA_MAX_BYTES (5 MB) is hardcoded and not user-configurable

summary.problem_summary: Sandbox media staging cap is hardcoded to 5 MB (const MEDIA_MAX_BYTES = 5 * 1024 * 1024 in dist/store-*.js), so files larger than 5 MB are rejected or silently fail when copied into sandboxed agents.
summary.solution_summary: Expose MEDIA_MAX_BYTES as a configurable value read from openclaw.json (for example tools.media.maxBytes) with a 5 MB default, and update store code to use that config before per-type checks.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Constant MEDIA_MAX_BYTES is defined in dist/store-*.js and has been hardcoded since at least v2026.3.7 (still present in v2026.3.8). Reproduce by running sandbox.mode: "non-main" and sending a file >5 MB. Temporary workaround: sed-patch dist/store-*.js after npm install. Suggested config key: tools.media.maxBytes; per-type keys tools.media.image.maxBytes and tools.media.audio.maxBytes already exist.
summary.dedupe_summary: Sandbox media staging limit MEDIA_MAX_BYTES hardcoded to 5 MB in dist/store-*.js and not configurable via openclaw.json, causing files >5MB to be rejected.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary of the core issue (hardcoded MEDIA_MAX_BYTES in dist/store-*.js, not configurable via openclaw.json, causing >5MB files to be rejected) with no extraneous boilerplate.

results.title: [Bug]: Discord REST API requests fail with "fetch failed" despite proxy configuration being enabled

summary.problem_summary: Discord bot receives messages via Gateway but all Discord REST API requests fail with "fetch failed" even though proxy is configured and logs show "rest proxy enabled"; curl using the same proxy succeeds and Telegram works with the same proxy.
summary.solution_summary: Fix the Discord REST client to honor the configured HTTP proxy (pass a proxy-aware agent or use a proxy-capable fetch), ensure the REST proxy code path isn't being skipped, and add tests/logging to validate REST requests use the proxy.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Gateway logs indicate "rest proxy enabled" and WebSocket (Gateway) connections work; REST operations (deploy commands, fetch bot identity, sending messages) return TypeError: fetch failed; direct curl requests through the same proxy succeed, isolating the failure to the app's Discord REST proxy path.
summary.dedupe_summary: Discord REST API requests fail with "fetch failed" despite rest proxy enabled and the same proxy working via curl

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary of the core issue—REST API calls erroring with 'fetch failed' despite proxy being enabled and usable via curl—no boilerplate or noisy details.

results.title: Discord REST API (@buape/carbon) ignores channels.discord.proxy config

summary.problem_summary: Discord REST API calls made via @buape/carbon's RequestClient (and related fetch uses like memorySearch and attachment downloads) ignore the channels.discord.proxy setting in openclaw.json; only the WebSocket gateway respects the proxy. RequestClient calls globalThis.fetch without a proxy-aware dispatcher, and Node.js 22's native fetch does not read HTTP(S)_PROXY env vars by default.
summary.solution_summary: Change @buape/carbon's RequestClient to accept an optional dispatcher/proxy configuration and have OpenClaw pass channels.discord.proxy to it so REST API calls use the same proxy as the WebSocket. As a temporary workaround, preload an undici EnvHttpProxyAgent via NODE_OPTIONS to set a global dispatcher, but avoid using --use-env-proxy because it breaks WebSocket connections.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: RequestClient currently invokes globalThis.fetch with no proxy dispatcher, causing REST requests, memorySearch, and some media downloads to bypass configured proxy; fix by exposing/accepting a dispatcher or proxy option and forwarding openclaw.json channels.discord.proxy to it. Do not recommend NODE_OPTIONS=--use-env-proxy because it interferes with WebSocket upgrades.
summary.dedupe_summary: @buape/carbon RequestClient uses globalThis.fetch without a proxy-aware dispatcher, so Discord REST requests ignore openclaw.json channels.discord.proxy (Node.js fetch doesn’t honor env proxy by default); add dispatcher/proxy support to RequestClient.

judge.reasoning: Concise and technical: it states that @buape/carbon's RequestClient uses globalThis.fetch (which doesn't respect proxy env) and causes Discord REST calls to ignore channels.discord.proxy, recommending dispatcher/proxy support.

results.title: [Bug]: Performance degradation - context growth + lane blocking under load

summary.problem_summary: OpenClaw shows performance degradation under sustained load with long-running conversations: unbounded context token growth (writer ~42k tokens, reasoning ~90k), Discord lane blocking causing serialization under load, and typing indicators stuck until TTL in some code paths. High concurrency settings and insufficient event queue timeouts exacerbate the issue.
summary.solution_summary: Reduced concurrency (acp.maxConcurrentSessions 12→6, agents.defaults.maxConcurrent 3→2, subagents maxConcurrent 8→4, maxChildrenPerAgent 8→4), set agents.defaults.contextTokens to 80000, and increased channels.discord.eventQueue.listenerTimeout from 120000 to 300000; improved patching script to cover all hash variants. Gateway restarted and errors (lane wait exceeded, typing TTL) ceased, though restarts are not a complete fix under sustained load.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Key observables: session token counts grew to ~42k (writer) and ~90k (reasoning), lane blocking and serialization by agentId under load, and typing indicators stuck until TTL. Applied config changes and restart reduced errors; recommend making context/concurrency limits adaptive or exposing them in openclaw config.
summary.dedupe_summary: Unbounded conversation context token growth plus high concurrency causes lane blocking/serialization and sticky typing indicators; mitigated by lowering maxConcurrentSessions/agent/subagent limits, setting agents.defaults.contextTokens=80000, and increasing Discord eventQueue listenerTimeout to 300000.

judge.reasoning: Concise, technical summary that captures the root causes (unbounded context growth and high concurrency causing lane blocking/typing issues) and the applied mitigations, making it well-suited for clustering similar reports.

results.title: [Bug] v2026.3.11: Discord thread context re-injected every turn (context pollution + cache bust)

summary.problem_summary: In OpenClaw v2026.3.11, Discord threads re-inject volatile metadata on every conversation turn (message_id, reply_to_id, timestamp, Thread starter, Chat history since last reply), causing context contamination, token growth, degraded performance, and prompt cache invalidation.
summary.solution_summary: Implement the fix in source: limit injection of thread-starter and volatile metadata to the first turn via an isNewSession guard, replace resolveAgentMainSessionKey with buildAgentMainSessionKey where needed, add tests and post-update validation, and rebuild so compiled dist no longer re-injects metadata.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Affected compiled files: dist/pi-embedded-D6PpOsxP.js (metadata injection) and dist/compact-1mmJ_KWL.js (missing isNewSession guard and wrong session-key call). Apply fix in source to stop per-turn injection of message_id/reply_to_id/timestamp/Thread starter/Chat history, add isNewSession guard, switch to buildAgentMainSessionKey(...), add tests and a post-update revalidation script; current manual dist patch is high-risk and will be overwritten on updates.
summary.dedupe_summary: Discord threads re-inject volatile metadata (message_id, reply_to_id, timestamp, Thread starter, Chat history since last reply) on every turn, causing context pollution and prompt-cache bust; compiled files dist/pi-embedded-D6PpOsxP.js and dist/compact-1mmJ_KWL.js inject metadata and lack an isNewSession guard.

judge.reasoning: Concise, technical summary that states the core bug (Discord threads re-inject volatile metadata every turn), lists the exact fields affected and implicated compiled files, and notes the missing session guard—suitable for clustering similar reports.

results.title: [Bug] v2026.3.11: Discord thread metadata re-injected every turn (context pollution)

summary.problem_summary: In OpenClaw v2026.3.11 Discord threads re-inject volatile metadata (message_id, reply_to_id, timestamp, Thread starter, Chat history since last reply) on every turn, causing context pollution, repeated prompt content, degraded performance on long threads, and prompt-cache busting that increases token costs.
summary.solution_summary: A manual patch was applied to the compiled artifact dist/pi-embedded-D6PpOsxP.js to stop re-injecting the listed metadata after the first turn; validation shows repeated context stopped, prompt caching restored, and token usage reduced. Recommend implementing the fix in source and adding a post-update revalidation to avoid losing the change on upgrades.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Affected file: dist/pi-embedded-D6PpOsxP.js; root cause is a missing guard that limits Discord thread metadata injection to the first turn (unlike Slack/iMessage). High regression risk because the fix is applied to compiled files—priority: commit source-level fix, add tests, and ensure upgrade-safe deployment or revalidation script.
summary.dedupe_summary: Discord threads re-inject message_id, reply_to_id, timestamp, Thread starter, and Chat history on every turn in v2026.3.11; patched in dist/pi-embedded-D6PpOsxP.js to inject this metadata only on the first turn.

judge.reasoning: Summary clearly states the core bug and lists the exact metadata re-injected (good for clustering), but includes version and patch/file details that are unnecessary for deduplication.

results.title: [Bug]: Discord threads root fix - runtime patches for context growth + session routing

summary.problem_summary: Two regressions in Discord thread handling: per-turn re-injection of metadata (message_id, reply_to_id, timestamp, Thread starter, InboundHistory) causing progressive context growth and a session resolver fallback to agent:*:main that routes CLI/session commands to the wrong session, leading to increased latency and degraded performance in long threads.
summary.solution_summary: Applied runtime patches to compiled dist bundles (dist/reply-BCcP6j4h.js, dist/compact-1mmJ_KWL.js, dist/pi-embedded-Cz5VjpnY.js, dist/gateway-cli-B-E8XzUM.js, dist/register.agent-jS8B5meo.js), restarted the gateway, created backups (*.bak-rootfix-20260313T023340Z) and an idempotent reapply script (/home/clawd/scripts/openclaw-rootfix-v2026.3.11.sh), and validated thread-bound session resolution and logs.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Manually patched compiled runtime bundles listed above; patches are not preserved across reinstalls so maintain the reapply script and backups. Gateway restarted (active) and logs show no new errors. Recommend adding smoke tests for long-thread context size growth and CLI routing correctness.
summary.dedupe_summary: Discord threads: per-turn metadata re-injection causing unbounded context growth and session resolver fallback to agent:*:main (wrong CLI/session routing); patched compiled dist bundles, added backups and a reapply script.

judge.reasoning: Summary accurately states the core bug (per-turn metadata re-injection causing unbounded context growth and session resolver fallback to agent:*:main) and the code area patched, though mentioning backups/reapply script adds minor operational noise not needed for dedupe.

results.title: [Bug]:  Discord messages not being sent - "fetch failed" despite proxy configuration

summary.problem_summary: OpenClaw receives Discord messages and generates responses, but sending the reply fails with "TypeError: fetch failed" even though Discord rest/gateway proxy is configured and logged as enabled and the same proxy works with curl.
summary.solution_summary: Ensure the Discord reply send path applies the configured HTTP proxy/agent to fetch calls (or reads proxy env) and add logging/tests to verify the fetch request uses the proxy; likely fix is to attach the proxy agent to REST requests in the reply code path.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Regression in OpenClaw 2026.3.2: bot logs show rest/gateway proxy enabled and successful login, messages are received and model responses generated, but reply sending errors with "TypeError: fetch failed"; curl through the proxy succeeds, indicating the reply REST requests are not using the configured proxy.
summary.dedupe_summary: Discord reply sending fails with "TypeError: fetch failed" despite configured and logged proxy; receiving messages works and curl via proxy succeeds, indicating the reply REST/fetch path isn't using the HTTP proxy.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific description of the core bug (Discord replies failing with fetch/proxy issue and REST path likely bypassing proxy) with no template noise, so it will cluster with similar reports.

results.title: [Bug] v2026.3.11: CLI agent --channel discord --to routes to agent:*:main instead of live session

summary.problem_summary: openclaw v2026.3.11 CLI 'openclaw agent --channel discord --to ...' resolves and delivers to agent:*:main instead of the live session for the specified Discord channel/thread because the session resolver does not lookup by delivery context and Discord targets are not normalized.
summary.solution_summary: Manually patched dist/gateway-cli-BjsM6fWb.js to normalize Discord targets ('148...' → 'channel:148...') and to resolve sessions by delivery context (channel+to+accountId) before falling back to agent:*:main; validated routing to correct session. Note: change is in compiled dist/ and must be ported to source to avoid loss on reinstall.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Implement delivery-context-based session lookup and Discord target normalization in source code (not just dist), add CLI routing tests, and replace the ad-hoc compiled-file patch to prevent regression on updates.
summary.dedupe_summary: CLI 'openclaw agent --channel discord --to ...' routes to agent:*:main because session resolver omits lookup by delivery context (channel+to+accountId) and fails to normalize Discord targets ('148...' vs 'channel:148...').

judge.reasoning: Concise and specific: it highlights the CLI routing to agent:*:main caused by the session resolver not looking up delivery context and failing to normalize Discord targets, which is exactly the core issue for clustering similar session-resolution/routing bugs.

results.title: [Bug] v2026.3.11: Regression in compact - resolveAgentMainSessionKey ReferenceError

summary.problem_summary: In OpenClaw v2026.3.11 a regression in the compiled file dist/compact-1mmJ_KWL.js calls an undefined function resolveAgentMainSessionKey, causing a ReferenceError, agent command failures, and potential gateway crashes.
summary.solution_summary: Manually patched dist/compact-1mmJ_KWL.js by replacing resolveAgentMainSessionKey(...) with buildAgentMainSessionKey(...); after the change the ReferenceError is gone and agent/session behavior is restored.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: This is a high-risk manual edit to compiled dist artifacts and will be lost on update; the correct fix must be applied to the source/build pipeline so the session-resolver patch and this replacement are both present in future builds.
summary.dedupe_summary: ReferenceError: resolveAgentMainSessionKey is not defined in dist/compact-1mmJ_KWL.js — replace with buildAgentMainSessionKey(...) to fix

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary of the runtime ReferenceError in the compiled file and the exact code change needed, with no extraneous boilerplate, so it will cluster correctly with similar issues.

results.title: [Bug]:  Discord plugin fails with "fetch failed" when using proxy

summary.problem_summary: Discord channel plugin shows ON | OK but health check and message send fail with 'fetch failed' when OpenClaw is behind a proxy; channels.discord.proxy is applied to WebSocket but not to REST API calls, while curl with the same proxy succeeds.
summary.solution_summary: Ensure the Discord plugin applies channels.discord.proxy to its HTTP/REST client (health check and message send) in addition to WebSocket connections; update client initialization, add tests, and verify health check uses the proxy.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Regression on OpenClaw 2026.3.8: configuring channels.discord.proxy does not route REST API requests through the proxy, causing health check 'fetch failed' and message send timeouts; curl with the proxy works, reproduces by configuring channels.discord.proxy and running openclaw message send.
summary.dedupe_summary: Discord plugin ignores channels.discord.proxy for REST API calls (health check and message send) — only WebSocket uses the proxy, causing 'fetch failed' in proxied environments.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary that captures the core bug (proxy not applied to REST API calls causing health check and sends to fail) without boilerplate or extraneous details.

results.title: [Bug] v2026.3.11: Session resolver in compact lacks delivery-context lookup

summary.problem_summary: In OpenClaw v2026.3.11 the session resolver in dist/compact-1mmJ_KWL.js for the agent command does not lookup by delivery context (channel + to + accountId), so sessions resolve to agent:*:main instead of the live session for the target channel, causing agent responses to be associated with the wrong session and routing inconsistencies with the CLI gateway.
summary.solution_summary: Manually patched dist/compact-1mmJ_KWL.js to add delivery-context lookup in the agent command session resolver (search by channel + to + accountId) and only fallback to main when no specific session is found; validation shows correct session resolution and response association.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: High regression risk: this is a manual edit to a compiled file in dist and will be lost on reinstall/update; the fix must be applied in source and rebuilt to be permanent and avoid future regressions.
summary.dedupe_summary: dist/compact-1mmJ_KWL.js session resolver missing delivery-context lookup (channel+to+accountId), causing default to agent:*:main; manual patch added delivery-context lookup but change exists only in compiled dist file.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary that identifies the affected compiled file, the missing delivery-context lookup (channel+to+accountId), the failure mode (falls back to agent:*:main), and that a manual patch exists only in the dist file.

results.title: [Feature Request] Add i18n support for OpenClaw Control UI (Chinese localization)

summary.problem_summary: The OpenClaw Control UI has most interface text hardcoded in English, creating usability barriers for non-English users, particularly Chinese speakers, despite the docs already having zh-CN translations.
summary.solution_summary: Extract all hardcoded UI strings into a structured i18n layer (e.g., JSON locale files), add a zh-CN locale file with translations, provide a language selector or auto-detect, and build infrastructure to add more languages.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Strings are currently hardcoded in the control-ui bundle (dist/control-ui/assets/index-*.js); docs/zh-CN exists and can be reused for translations. Recommended scope: extract strings, add zh-CN, add UI language selector/auto-detect, and update build to load locales.
summary.dedupe_summary: Extract hardcoded English strings from OpenClaw Control UI into locale files and add zh-CN translations plus a language selector/auto-detect.

judge.reasoning: Concise and specific: it states extracting hardcoded strings to locale files, adding zh-CN translations, and introducing language selection/auto-detect, which accurately captures the i18n feature request for the OpenClaw Control UI.

results.title: feat(whatsapp): add systemPrompt support for accounts and groups

summary.problem_summary: WhatsApp group chats could not apply channel-configured systemPrompt instructions at account or group granularity, preventing per-account or per-group reply customization and wildcard defaults.
summary.solution_summary: Added systemPrompt support to WhatsApp config/schema at channels.whatsapp, accounts.<id>, and groups.<groupId> (including wildcard "*"); implemented account resolution, group prompt composition, tests and docs; account-level values override root and account.groups fully replace root groups; final injected prompt is account prompt plus resolved group prompt separated by a blank line.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Change scope limited to WhatsApp: no changes to non-WhatsApp channels, hot-reload behavior, or group allowlist/mention-gating semantics; includes clear resolution hierarchy (account prompt > group prompt, specific group > wildcard, account.groups replaces root groups).
summary.dedupe_summary: WhatsApp: add systemPrompt hierarchy — support systemPrompt at channels.whatsapp, accounts.<id>, and groups.<groupId> (with "*" wildcard); account-level overrides root and account.groups replace root groups; final prompt = account prompt + resolved group prompt.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary of the new WhatsApp systemPrompt resolution hierarchy and composition that captures the core change and would match similar PRs/issues.

results.title: Fix WhatsApp group append processing and account-scoped gatin

summary.problem_summary: WhatsApp group messages delivered via messages.upsert(type="append") were being dropped before agent processing, and WhatsApp group policy resolution could ignore account-scoped config (causing allowlist/require-mention checks to fail).
summary.solution_summary: Append processing is enabled only for WhatsApp group messages, and group policy resolution now propagates accountId and uses account-scoped groupAllowFrom/allowFrom fallbacks so account-scoped WhatsApp settings apply; DM append history remains skipped.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Change touches WhatsApp inbound and group-activation code (src/web/inbound/monitor.ts, src/web/auto-reply/monitor/group-activation.ts), was validated against a live OpenClaw WhatsApp deployment with trace logs showing before/after behavior, adds targeted tests for account-scoped allowlist fallback, and makes no config/schema/debug/logging or unrelated channel changes.
summary.dedupe_summary: Enable messages.upsert(type="append") processing for WhatsApp groups and use accountId to apply account-scoped groupAllowFrom/allowFrom when resolving WhatsApp group policy defaults.

judge.reasoning: Concise, focused summary that captures the core change—enabling append processing for WhatsApp groups and using account-scoped accountId for group policy resolution—without extraneous boilerplate.

results.title: fix(gateway): resolve Canvas 401 behind Tailscale Serve

summary.problem_summary: Canvas (A2UI) returned 401 when accessed via Tailscale Serve because the gateway treated requests from the trusted proxy as unauthenticated: resolveClientIp() returned undefined when the proxy sent no forwarding headers, and isLocalDirectRequest() did not fall back to the raw socket address when XFF resolved to a non-loopback Tailscale IP.
summary.solution_summary: Add socket-address fallback in resolveClientIp() when no forwarding headers are present; have isLocalDirectRequest() check req.socket.remoteAddress when allowRealIpFallback is enabled; gate .ts.net host acceptance behind the existing allowTailscale flag and thread auth.allowTailscale through callers; update docs and tests.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Changes touch gateway auth/net/server code and tests (src/gateway/{auth.ts,net.ts,server-*.ts}, session-kill-http) and docs; tests added and passing. Behavior change only when no XFF/X-Real-IP headers are present or when allowRealIpFallback is enabled; allowTailscale defaults to true for tailscale.mode = "serve" to preserve existing setups. Risk: different callers may now see socket IP when proxies omit forwarding headers; mitigation and docs provided.
summary.dedupe_summary: Canvas returned 401 behind Tailscale Serve because resolveClientIp returned undefined for direct trusted-proxy requests without XFF and isLocalDirectRequest didn’t fall back to socket.remoteAddress; fix adds socket fallback and gates .ts.net host acceptance behind allowTailscale.

judge.reasoning: Concise, technical summary that states the root cause (no socket fallback causing 401 behind Tailscale Serve) and the precise fixes (socket.remoteAddress fallback and gating .ts.net via allowTailscale), so it will cluster with similar issues.

results.title: fix(security): block non-self messages in WhatsApp self-chat mode

summary.problem_summary: checkInboundAccessControl computed an isSelfChat heuristic but never used it as a guard, allowing non-self WhatsApp messages (group messages, DMs from strangers, broadcasts) to pass through because groupPolicy defaulted to "open", violating self-chat isolation when selfChatMode was intended to be absolute.
summary.solution_summary: Introduce an early-return guard in checkInboundAccessControl that rejects all non-self messages when account.selfChatMode === true; the guard runs before group/DM policy evaluation and intentionally uses the explicit config flag (not the isSelfChat heuristic) to avoid blocking teammates in mixed allowFrom lists.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Guard is placed before group and DM policy resolution and preserves isSelfChat in the result for UI/logging; auto-detected self-chat via heuristic is not hard-blocked by design; tests pass and the change can be reverted by removing the early-return guard.
summary.dedupe_summary: Explicit selfChatMode true did not block non-self WhatsApp messages because isSelfChat was computed but unused and groupPolicy defaulted to "open"; fix adds an early-return that rejects non-self messages when account.selfChatMode === true.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary that identifies the bug (isSelfChat computed but unused, groupPolicy defaulting to open) and the exact fix (early-return rejecting non-self messages when account.selfChatMode === true), so it will cluster correctly with similar changes.

results.title: fix: store group messages from non-allowlisted senders as pending context

summary.problem_summary: With groupPolicy: "allowlist", group messages from senders not in groupAllowFrom are dropped at the access-control layer (allowed: false → continue) and never stored for context, so the agent cannot see those messages in its "[Chat messages since your last reply]" context block and documented mention-gating behavior is not met.
summary.solution_summary: Introduce an opt-in groupContextFromAll flag (top-level and per-account, per-account wins) defaulting to false; when enabled, access-control returns storeForContext for non-allowlisted group senders, inbound monitor marks them as contextOnly (skips read receipts) and records them in group history as pending context without triggering replies; added types, schema, and account config changes while preserving backward compatibility.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Changes touch src/web/inbound/access-control.ts, monitor.ts, types.ts, auto-reply/on-message.ts, config types and zod schema, and accounts resolution. Potential risks: allowlist normalization/matching may misclassify senders, and the new context-only early path bypasses some existing group gating/state updates, which could cause subtle inconsistencies.
summary.dedupe_summary: Add groupContextFromAll option to store non-allowlisted WhatsApp group messages as pending context (contextOnly) instead of dropping them when groupPolicy is allowlist.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary of the core change—new groupContextFromAll option to store non-allowlisted WhatsApp group messages as pending context instead of dropping them—without boilerplate.

results.title: feat(sessions_spawn): add sessionKey param to reuse sub-agent sessions

summary.problem_summary: sessions_spawn always created a new random-UUID session for each sub-agent spawn, preventing reuse of conversation history and enabling workarounds that bypass allowlists.
summary.solution_summary: Add an optional sessionKey parameter so sub-agents can run in a deterministic session. Short keys map to agent:{agentId}:subagent:{key}; fully-qualified keys containing ":subagent:" are validated to match the targetAgentId to prevent cross-agent session injection; omission falls back to crypto.randomUUID(). Includes four tests for deterministic key, UUID fallback, fully-qualified passthrough, and cross-agent mismatch rejection.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Blocking test import: a test file imports a non-existent createOpenClawTools from ./openclaw-tools.js instead of the correct ./clawdbot-tools.js, causing compile failure. Case-sensitivity bug: embedded agentId extracted from fully-qualified keys is not normalized/lowercased while targetAgentId is normalized, causing false mismatch rejections. Inconsistent error handling: cross-agent mismatch uses throw new Error(...) while other security checks return jsonResult(...), producing different error surfaces.
summary.dedupe_summary: sessions_spawn now accepts an optional sessionKey to reuse deterministic sub-agent sessions; short keys map to agent:{agentId}:subagent:{key}, fully-qualified keys are validated to match targetAgentId to prevent cross-agent session injection, otherwise falls back to crypto.randomUUID().

judge.reasoning: Concise, technical summary that captures the core change (optional sessionKey for deterministic sub-agent sessions), mapping rules, validation to prevent cross-agent injection, and fallback behavior without template noise.

results.title: fix(telegram): auto-detect captionable messages for editMessageCaption

summary.problem_summary: Editing Telegram messages that contain media (photo, video, audio, document, animation) via the generic edit path calls editMessageText and fails with 400: "there is no text in the message to edit", causing message edits to silently error and leave the UI inconsistent.
summary.solution_summary: editMessageTelegram now detects that error via a NO_TEXT_IN_MESSAGE_RE regex, retries the edit with editMessageCaption for captionable media, routes buttons-only updates to editMessageReplyMarkup, preserves HTML parse fallback, and treats MESSAGE_NOT_MODIFIED as success; no public API or config changes.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Changes live in src/telegram/send.ts: added NO_TEXT_IN_MESSAGE_RE and retry logic from editMessageText → editMessageCaption, plus direct editMessageReplyMarkup path for buttons-only updates; six unit tests added covering retry, buttons-only, error propagation, and MESSAGE_NOT_MODIFIED handling; backward compatible and only affects the Telegram adapter; risk: regex may need updates if Telegram changes error wording.
summary.dedupe_summary: Telegram editMessageText returns 400 'there is no text in the message to edit' for media messages; fix retries with editMessageCaption and uses editMessageReplyMarkup for buttons-only updates.

judge.reasoning: Concise, technical summary that states the specific Telegram API error and the implemented retry/fallback (editMessageCaption and editMessageReplyMarkup), with no boilerplate or extraneous details.

results.title: fix(whatsapp): restore config-driven block streaming for WhatsApp delivery

summary.problem_summary: PR #24962 hardcoded disableBlockStreaming:true for WhatsApp to address a reasoning-content leak, which unintentionally disabled block streaming entirely (messages were batched and sent at the end) and ignored per-account and global block streaming config.
summary.solution_summary: Changed the deliver callback to only suppress tool payloads and made disableBlockStreaming driven by per-account channels.whatsapp.blockStreaming with a fallback to agents.defaults.blockStreamingDefault; upstream reasoning suppression remains in place.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Reasoning/thinking content is still filtered upstream by shouldSuppressReasoningPayload; tool payloads remain suppressed; tests (including live WhatsApp verification and inbound contract tests) pass; default behavior unchanged unless explicitly configured.
summary.dedupe_summary: WhatsApp block streaming was hardcoded off (disableBlockStreaming:true), ignoring channels.whatsapp.blockStreaming and agents.defaults.blockStreamingDefault; this change restores config-driven block streaming and limits deliver suppression to tool payloads while keeping reasoning suppression upstream.

judge.reasoning: Concise, technical summary that captures the core bug (hardcoded disableBlockStreaming for WhatsApp), the config-driven restoration, and the narrowed deliver suppression to tool payloads while noting reasoning filtering remains upstream.

results.title: fix(telegram): fix gateway hang on video messages and increase media download limit

summary.problem_summary: Sending videos larger than the previous 5MB default caused the Telegram gateway to hang: fetchRemoteMedia hit a content-length > maxBytes and closeDispatcher (undici Agent.close) deadlocked when the response body was unconsumed, leaving the gateway unresponsive and previously dropping messages when media failed.
summary.solution_summary: Raised default channels.telegram.mediaMaxMb from 5 to 20, fixed closeDispatcher to timeout and fall back to destroy to avoid Agent.close deadlocks, added timeouts and retry logic to getFile and downloads, made media download errors non-fatal so text/captions are forwarded, and extended UI metadata stripping for inbound media paths.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Changes touch src/infra/net/ssrf.ts and src/telegram/bot.ts; default mediaMaxMb is now 20 but remains configurable via channels.telegram.mediaMaxMb; no SSRF policy, media storage, or agent-side processing changes; strip-inbound-meta tests updated and pass.
summary.dedupe_summary: Telegram gateway hung on video messages >5MB due to undici Agent.close deadlock when response body was unconsumed; fix increases channels.telegram.mediaMaxMb to 20, adds timeouts/retries for media download, makes media errors non-fatal, and forces Agent.close to timeout then destroy.

judge.reasoning: Concise and technical summary that states the root cause (undici Agent.close deadlock) and the specific fixes (increase mediaMaxMb, timeouts/retries, non-fatal media errors, forced Agent.close destruction), with no boilerplate — ideal for deduplication.

results.title: fix(telegram): use type-aware media placeholder for document attachments (#7116)

summary.problem_summary: Telegram document attachments sent without caption were always represented as "<media:image>" in the agent-facing inbound body, mislabeling documents (PDF/DOCX/etc.) as images and causing agents to respond as if no document was received.
summary.solution_summary: Changed resolveTelegramInboundBody to use the precomputed type-aware placeholder (e.g. "<media:document>") instead of a hardcoded "<media:image>" for non-audio media; added unit tests covering placeholders for all media types and a test verifying document messages trigger getFile.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Scope limited to the agent-facing fallback body text in Telegram integration; media download, understanding pipeline, and file extraction unchanged. Tests added in extensions/telegram/src/bot/helpers.test.ts and extensions/telegram/src/bot.create-telegram-bot.test.ts. Risk: downstream code that pattern-matches only "<media:image>" might be affected, but this was an edge-case fallback and the change is more correct.
summary.dedupe_summary: Telegram inbound-body fallback used hardcoded "<media:image>" for non-audio media, causing documents to be labeled as images; fixed to use the type-aware placeholder so document attachments produce "<media:document>".

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary that captures the bug (hardcoded <media:image> placeholder) and the fix (use type-aware placeholder producing <media:document>), with relevant technical terms and no boilerplate.

results.title: media: make MEDIA_MAX_BYTES configurable via media.maxBytes

summary.problem_summary: MEDIA_MAX_BYTES was hardcoded to 5 MB in src/media/store.ts, causing files larger than 5 MB to fail to stage into sandboxed agents and making common file types unusable in sandboxed workflows.
summary.solution_summary: Introduced a new media.maxBytes config field, added getMediaMaxBytes()/setMediaMaxBytes() accessors and wired gateway boot to read and apply the config, updated error messages to show the configured limit; default remains 5 MB and schema caps values at 500 MB.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: All relevant tests pass (56 tests across four files). Backwards compatible: MEDIA_MAX_BYTES exported (deprecated) and default preserved. Schema enforces 500 MB max; fixed previous issues with unset config and MB formatting; risk of disk fill noted and mitigated by TTL and schema cap.
summary.dedupe_summary: Make MEDIA_MAX_BYTES configurable via media.maxBytes; replace hardcoded 5MB staging limit with a runtime-configurable limit (default 5MB, maximum 500MB) so sandboxed agents can accept larger files.

judge.reasoning: Clear, concise technical summary that captures the core change (replace hardcoded 5MB staging limit with media.maxBytes config and default/maximum values) without template noise, so it will cluster with similar issues.

results.title: fix: pass file metadata to agent when Telegram media download fails

summary.problem_summary: When Telegram media download fails due to exceeding size limits or a Telegram API error, the handler returned early and the entire message (including caption/text) was silently dropped; the agent received no ack and the user got no indication their file was lost.
summary.solution_summary: On download failure, extract file metadata (name, size, type) from the Telegram message object and inject a metadata stub into msg.text instead of returning early, allowing the message to continue through the normal pipeline so the agent sees the file and reason for failure.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Change implemented in src/telegram/bot-handlers.ts: buildFileMetadataStub populates msg.text on error paths for files over mediaMaxMb or Telegram's 20MB getFile limit; normal media download, media groups, sticker handling, and outbound sending are unchanged. No new network calls or permissions; risk is mutating msg.text only on previously-dropped messages and can be reverted by reverting the single commit to bot-handlers.ts.
summary.dedupe_summary: Telegram media download failures (file too large or API error) previously caused the message to be dropped; now file metadata stub is injected into msg.text so the agent receives the file and failure reason.

judge.reasoning: Concise and specific: states the bug (Telegram media download failures dropped messages) and the exact fix (inject file metadata stub into msg.text so the agent sees the file and failure reason), which will match similar issues.

results.title: feat(cli): add --session-key targeting for openclaw agent

summary.problem_summary: The openclaw agent CLI lacked a stable targeting option, causing CLI-triggered deliveries and callback automation to resolve to the agent main session because sessionId is UUID-based and --to or --agent are unreliable in some environments.
summary.solution_summary: Added a new --session-key CLI flag, extended AgentCliOpts with sessionKey, forwarded it into resolveSessionKeyForRequest and the gateway call, updated help/validation text, and added tests to verify parsing and forwarding. Session resolution precedence is now session-key > session-id > to > agent.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Safe, backward-compatible change touching src/cli/program/register.agent.ts and src/commands/agent-via-gateway.ts, adds tests for CLI parsing and gateway parameter forwarding, minimal risk and straightforward to review and merge.
summary.dedupe_summary: Add --session-key flag to openclaw agent CLI; forward sessionKey through AgentCliOpts into resolveSessionKeyForRequest so session targeting uses session-key over session-id/to/agent.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary that captures the core change—adding a --session-key CLI flag and plumbing it through AgentCliOpts to resolveSessionKeyForRequest to prioritize session-key targeting—without extraneous boilerplate.

results.title: feat: separate group-level allowlist from sender-level command authorization

summary.problem_summary: With groupPolicy set to "allowlist", the system currently gates all group interactions (chat and slash commands) by sender phone number via groupAllowFrom, preventing: allowing specific groups while letting anyone chat, restricting slash commands to owners only, and blocking spam groups while keeping approved groups open.
summary.solution_summary: When groupPolicy is "allowlist" and a channels.whatsapp.groups mapping exists, inbound access is gated by the groups JID allowlist (only listed groups accepted) while anyone in an approved group may chat; groupAllowFrom continues to control slash-command authorization. If no groups mapping is present, behavior falls back to the legacy sender-based filtering.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Changes localized to src/web/inbound/access-control.ts (use resolveChannelGroupPolicy), updated JSDoc in src/config/types.whatsapp.ts, and four new Vitest tests for group allowlist behavior; depends on PR #3326 to require explicit groupPolicy: "allowlist" when using groups config and includes a minor doc/comment update.
summary.dedupe_summary: WhatsApp: groupPolicy "allowlist" now uses channels.whatsapp.groups JID mapping as the group-level allowlist (allows any participant to chat in approved groups) while groupAllowFrom still limits slash commands; falls back to sender-based filtering when no groups mapping is configured.

judge.reasoning: Concise and specific: it captures the core change to use the channels.whatsapp.groups JID mapping as the group-level allowlist, the resulting open-chat behavior, that groupAllowFrom still gates slash commands, and the sender-filtering fallback.

results.title: fix(telegram): propagate mediaMaxMb limit to outbound media reply delivery

summary.problem_summary: Telegram's configured mediaMaxMb limit was ignored for outbound reply messages because loadWebMedia in the reply delivery path received no maxBytes and fell back to the default 100 MB, so smaller operator limits had no effect.
summary.solution_summary: Propagated mediaMaxBytes through the Telegram outbound reply delivery chain (bot.ts → bot-message.ts → bot-message-dispatch.ts → delivery.replies.ts) and the native-commands path (bot-native-commands.ts) so loadWebMedia receives the configured maxBytes; added unit tests and kept behavior unchanged when unset.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Direct-send path in send.ts was already correct; this change only threads mediaMaxBytes through reply and native-commands delivery paths, is backward compatible (mediaMaxBytes optional), and unit tests covering the change pass.
summary.dedupe_summary: Telegram outbound reply delivery ignored telegramAccounts.mediaMaxMb (loadWebMedia got no maxBytes and defaulted to 100MB); mediaMaxBytes is now threaded through bot.ts, bot-message.ts, bot-message-dispatch.ts, delivery.replies.ts and bot-native-commands.ts so replies enforce the configured limit.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary of the bug (reply delivery ignored mediaMaxMb) and the fix (threaded mediaMaxBytes through the listed Telegram outbound delivery files) with no template noise, so it will cluster correctly with similar issues.

results.title: fix(telegram): add HTML parse fallback for media captions

summary.problem_summary: Telegram media send functions (photo, document, video, animation, audio, voice, video note) lacked the HTML-parse fallback used by text messages, so captions with invalid HTML/entities caused the whole media send to fail with a 400 "can't parse entities" error.
summary.solution_summary: Wrapped the media-send callback in withTelegramHtmlParseFallback so on an HTML parse error the send is retried without parse_mode and with the raw caption; this applies to all media types and a unit test verifies the initial failure and subsequent retry behavior. No changes were made to text message, edit message, thread fallback, or caption-splitting logic.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Change made in src/telegram/send.ts; added unit test 'retries media caption as plain text on HTML parse error' and all tests pass. Backward-compatible; revert by removing the fallback wrapper; risk is only that fallback sends raw text instead of rendered HTML.
summary.dedupe_summary: Telegram media sends (sendPhoto/sendDocument/sendVideo/sendAnimation/sendAudio/sendVoice/video note) failed with 400 "can't parse entities" for captions containing invalid HTML; now retries by removing parse_mode and sending caption as plain text.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary of the core bug and fix naming affected APIs and error and omitting boilerplate, so it will cluster correctly with similar reports.

results.title: fix(whatsapp): make block streaming configurable and fix delivery race in block-reply pipeline

summary.problem_summary: WhatsApp had block streaming effectively disabled and a delivery race in the block-reply pipeline that could suppress both intermediate blocks and the final reply. disableBlockStreaming was hardcoded and its assignment used undefined which prevented activation, and onBlockReply used a sync enqueue so the pipeline marked sentContentKeys before actual delivery.
summary.solution_summary: Make block streaming configurable via channels.whatsapp.blockStreaming; fix disableBlockStreaming assignment; add blockStreamingCoalesceDefaults; implement dispatcher.sendBlockReplyAsync and await it in onBlockReply so sentContentKeys are only marked after confirmed delivery; update tests for streaming-on and streaming-off paths.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Key changes: process-message.ts now resolves account blockStreaming and fixes disableBlockStreaming assignment; shared.ts adds coalesce defaults; reply-dispatcher.ts adds sendBlockReplyAsync with per-item settlement; dispatch-from-config.ts awaits sendBlockReplyAsync; tests updated and new cases added to cover streaming enabled/disabled. This fixes the root cause of silent turns by ensuring block delivery is confirmed before marking sent.
summary.dedupe_summary: WhatsApp block streaming race: onBlockReply used sync sendBlockReply causing pipeline to mark sentContentKeys before confirmed delivery and suppress final replies; fixed by making blockStreaming configurable, correcting disableBlockStreaming handling, and adding sendBlockReplyAsync with awaiting of confirmed delivery.

judge.reasoning: Concise, technical summary that identifies the core race condition in WhatsApp block-reply handling, the incorrect disableBlockStreaming handling, and the implemented fix (awaiting delivery via sendBlockReplyAsync), so it would cluster with similar issues.

results.title: fix(signal): add group-level allowlist support via groups config

summary.problem_summary: Signal channel lacked a group-level allowlist option; groups could not be explicitly allowed by ID and thus were still subject to sender-level groupAllowFrom checks and could not run control commands.
summary.solution_summary: Added channels.signal.groups.<groupId> config and SignalGroupConfig type, extended Zod schema, wired resolveChannelGroupPolicy into the Signal event handler to allow group-level bypass for message delivery and command authorization, and updated docs with examples.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Implementation follows existing patterns used by other channels, preserves backward compatibility via hasGroupAllowFrom, TypeScript compilation passes, and the change was manually tested; safe to merge.
summary.dedupe_summary: Add channels.signal.groups.<groupId> allowlist to bypass sender-level groupAllowFrom and permit control commands for explicitly allowed Signal groups.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary describing the new Signal groups allowlist that bypasses sender-level checks and enables command authorization for allowed groups, which is exactly the core change for deduplication.

results.title: fix(signal): add group-level allowlist support via groups config

summary.problem_summary: Signal was the only channel missing group-level allowlisting via channels.signal.groups.<groupId>, so when groupPolicy was set to "allowlist" and groupAllowFrom contained group IDs, group messages were silently dropped because groupAllowFrom is a sender-level filter and group IDs never matched sender IDs.
summary.solution_summary: Added a groups config to SignalAccountSchemaBase and updated Signal's event handler to call resolveChannelGroupPolicy before falling back to sender-level groupAllowFrom; added four tests and verified existing tests pass.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Signal lacked the groups config and the resolveChannelGroupPolicy access check used by other channels; fix adds the groups schema and invokes resolveChannelGroupPolicy in Signal's group access path so group IDs in channels.signal.groups.<groupId> permit group messages.
summary.dedupe_summary: Signal lacked group-level allowlist support (channels.signal.groups.<groupId>); groupAllowFrom is sender-level so group IDs never matched; added groups config and resolveChannelGroupPolicy call to allow group-level allowlisting.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary that identifies the bug (missing group-level allowlist for Signal due to misuse of sender-level groupAllowFrom) and the fix (added groups config and resolveChannelGroupPolicy), so it will match similar issues precisely.

results.title: feat(whatsapp): support config-driven block streaming

summary.problem_summary: WhatsApp messages are being batched because process-message.ts hardcodes disableBlockStreaming: true, causing intermediate agent blocks to accumulate and be sent as one final payload.
summary.solution_summary: Replace the hardcoded disableBlockStreaming with a config-driven resolveWhatsAppBlockStreamingEnabled helper, register blockStreaming capability and DEFAULT_BLOCK_STREAMING_COALESCE in the WhatsApp dock, and fix queuedFinal logic so intermediate block payloads count as delivered; tests updated to cover enabled/disabled behavior.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: When channels.whatsapp.blockStreaming is true, intermediate blocks are delivered as separate WhatsApp messages immediately; when false or omitted, behavior remains backward-compatible with a single final reply. Change aligns WhatsApp behavior with Telegram/Line.
summary.dedupe_summary: Respect channels.whatsapp.blockStreaming instead of hardcoded disableBlockStreaming:true so intermediate blocks are delivered as separate WhatsApp messages; fix queuedFinal marking and register the capability/default.

judge.reasoning: Concise, focused summary that captures the core change—making WhatsApp block streaming config-driven, fixing queuedFinal delivery behavior, and registering the capability—without template noise.
